title: The Corporate AI Learning Curve Is Flatlining - Paid Program
search_path_label: A 级媒体 / GDELT 路径
full_text:
By Malte Kosub, Co-Founder and CEO of Parloa
The corporate rush into artificial intelligence has triggered a push to invest more in the technology. The appeal is obvious: AI promises to do more with less, automating routine work while freeing employees to focus on higher-value tasks.
And nowhere is that promise more compelling than in customer service. AI agents can take on enormous volumes of requests, resolve issues in seconds and operate around the clock without fatigue. For executives, it offers the holy grail of efficiency—lower costs and a better customer experience—at the same time. It’s no wonder companies have scrambled to roll out chatbots, voice assistants and AI-driven support flows.
But this is where the illusion sets in. None of that guarantees business outcomes. Notably, the most dangerous misconception in the boardroom is that AI agents are intuitive; that employees can simply “pick it up” and value will follow.
It doesn’t.
The blind spot isn’t the technology, but the way leaders expect people to learn how to use it, confusing exposure with mastery. Learners advance fastest when given just enough structure to attempt a task slightly beyond their ability. As competence grows, the support is gradually withdrawn.
However, most enterprises are skipping this step entirely. Service teams are dropped into technological experimentation and expected to understand the technology without guidance, benchmarks or examples.
AI Agent Management Is the Skill Your Team Needs
What teams need then isn’t more exposure, but more structured training. In practice, the biggest challenge is learning how to direct AI agents. They need direction, oversight and integration, just like employees. And without that discipline, companies see stalled pilots, frustrated service teams and customer experiences that suffer.
It helps to think about how you onboard a new employee. You don’t just walk them to a desk and hope for the best. You set priorities, help them practice how to face customers, review their early work, and define how they mesh with the rest of the team.
Time and again, the technology itself hasn’t been the constraint; it’s how companies invest in their people’s ability to harness it.
Shifting Perspective
The same thinking applies when the “new employee” isn’t human at all—instead, it’s an AI agent, and the team’s job is to learn how to train it. That means learning how to set performance goals during the design stage so the system knows what “good” looks like. It means running scenario tests so agents can fail safely before serving live customers. It requires evaluation processes that flag the right cases for human review instead of drowning staff in edge cases. And it means orchestration: teaching multiple agents how to cooperate and escalate to people when needed.
These are management skills for a new class of workforce. But here’s the catch: Time and again, the technology itself hasn’t been the constraint; it’s how companies invest in their people’s ability to harness it.
That’s why, at Parloa , we created Parloa Academy. Instead of hoping that exposure alone would close the skills gap, we put every employee—engineers, product, customer success, even non-technical staff—through a hands-on learning pathway during onboarding. This means they don’t just “use” AI agents; they build them from the ground up on our platform. We immerse teams in setting realistic goals, designing scenarios, running real tests and iterating based on what they learn.
The result is fluency in managing, troubleshooting and improving AI agents as part of cross-functional teams. It’s this deliberate, structured kind of team learning that keeps companies’ growth curve climbing instead of flatlining.
Because in the end, companies that embrace this mindset will turn experiments with AI into ROI. Whereas companies that don’t will stay stuck, watching investments underperform while competitors scale. For enterprise leaders, the takeaway is clear: The future of agentic AI won’t be decided by better models, but by better-prepared teams.
Get practical insights on how AI agents can elevate the customer experience.

The corporate rush into artificial intelligence has triggered a push to invest more in the technology. The appeal is obvious: AI promises to do more with less, automating routine work while freeing employees to focus on higher-value tasks.
And nowhere is that promise more compelling than in customer service. AI agents can take on enormous volumes of requests, resolve issues in seconds and operate around the clock without fatigue. For executives, it offers the holy grail of efficiency—lower costs and a better customer experience—at the same time. It’s no wonder companies have scrambled to roll out chatbots, voice assistants and AI-driven support flows.
But this is where the illusion sets in. None of that guarantees business outcomes. Notably, the most dangerous misconception in the boardroom is that AI agents are intuitive; that employees can simply “pick it up” and value will follow.
It doesn’t.
The blind spot isn’t the technology, but the way leaders expect people to learn how to use it, confusing exposure with mastery. Learners advance fastest when given just enough structure to attempt a task slightly beyond their ability. As competence grows, the support is gradually withdrawn.
However, most enterprises are skipping this step entirely. Service teams are dropped into technological experimentation and expected to understand the technology without guidance, benchmarks or examples.
AI Agent Management Is the Skill Your Team Needs
What teams need then isn’t more exposure, but more structured training. In practice, the biggest challenge is learning how to direct AI agents. They need direction, oversight and integration, just like employees. And without that discipline, companies see stalled pilots, frustrated service teams and customer experiences that suffer.
It helps to think about how you onboard a new employee. You don’t just walk them to a desk and hope for the best. You set priorities, help them practice how to face customers, review their early work, and define how they mesh with the rest of the team.
Time and again, the technology itself hasn’t been the constraint; it’s how companies invest in their people’s ability to harness it.

The same thinking applies when the “new employee” isn’t human at all—instead, it’s an AI agent, and the team’s job is to learn how to train it. That means learning how to set performance goals during the design stage so the system knows what “good” looks like. It means running scenario tests so agents can fail safely before serving live customers. And it means orchestration: teaching multiple agents how to cooperate and escalate to people when needed.
These are management skills for a new class of workforce. But here’s the catch: Time and again, the technology itself hasn’t been the constraint; it’s how companies invest in their people’s ability to harness it.
That’s why, at Parloa , we created Parloa Academy. Instead of hoping that exposure alone would close the skills gap, we put every employee—engineers, product, customer success, even non-technical staff—through a hands-on learning pathway during onboarding. This means they don’t just “use” AI agents; they build them from the ground up on our platform.
